Source: Levenstein, S., Prantera, C., Varvo, V., Scribano, M. L., Berto, E., Luzi, C., and Andreoli, A. (1993). Development of the Perceived Stress Questionnaire: a new tool for psychosomatic research. Journal of psychosomatic research, 37(1), 19-32. DOI: 10.23668/psycharchives.2889

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Social Acceptance: Wearable Acceptability Range (WEAR Scale v.3)
method.p: Description: The WEAR scale (v.3) is a measure that has been developed to understand the factors that affect the social acceptability of wearable devices and to predict their acceptance. The development of the scale involved defining the construct "social acceptability of a wearable" and creating an initial item pool, which was reviewed by experts. The resulting scale was administered to sample respondents along with similar scales for validation purposes. Factor analysis resulted in a two-factor, fourteen-item solution (WEAR v.3) that was consistent among the three datasets. The WEAR v.3 scale demonstrates good reliability and construct validity, and provides valuable information for academia and industry, including predictive modeling, theory-building, and wearable development and applications. The factors affecting the social acceptability of wearable devices include manners, moral codes, the symbolic communication of dress, habits of dress, fashion, context of use, form, and aesthetics.
Source: Kelly, Norene, The WEAR Scale: Development of a measure of the social acceptability of a wearable device (2016). Graduate Theses and Dissertations. 15230.
Download: The WEAR Scale (PhD Thesis by Norene Kelly, Final WEAR Scale v.3 can be found on p. 176)

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Human-Likeness: Uncanny Valley Questionnaire (UVQ)
method.p: Description: The Uncanny Valley Questionnaire (UVQ) is a psychological instrument that is used to measure the human response to humanoid robots or other artificial agents that resemble humans but are not quite perfect. The "uncanny valley" refers to the idea that as a robot or artificial agent becomes more human-like in appearance and behavior, there is a point at which it becomes eerily similar to a human but not quite human enough, causing a sense of revulsion or discomfort in humans. The UVQ is designed to assess this effect by asking participants to rate the familiarity, human-likeness, and eeriness of different artificial agents, including robots, virtual characters, and prosthetic limbs. The UVQ has been used in various studies to investigate the relationship between human responses and different aspects of the appearance and behavior of humanoid robots, and it has proven to be a useful tool for understanding the psychological factors that influence the acceptance and adoption of these types of technologies.
Source: Chin-Chang, H., and MacDorman, K. F. (2017). Measuring the Uncanny Valley Effect. International Journal of Social Robotics, 9(1), 129-139. DOI: 10.1007/s12369-016-0380-9
Download: Measuring the Uncanny Valley Effect (Table 2)

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Social Acceptance: Stereotype Content Model (SCM)
method.p: Description: The stereotype content model (SCM) is a social psychological theory that proposes that people form stereotypes about social groups based on two primary dimensions: warmth and competence. Warmth refers to how friendly and well-intentioned a group is perceived to be, while competence refers to how capable and skilled a group is perceived to be. According to the SCM, people's perceptions of social groups along these dimensions influence their attitudes and behaviors towards those groups. Specifically, groups that are perceived as warm and competent are likely to be highly regarded and socially accepted, while groups that are perceived as cold and incompetent are likely to be marginalized and discriminated against. For example, the stereotype that women are warm but less competent than men can lead to their exclusion from leadership positions in certain domains. Similarly, the stereotype that certain racial or ethnic groups are less competent can lead to discrimination in hiring or educational opportunities. Overall, the SCM helps to explain how stereotypes and social acceptance are linked, and it underscores the importance of addressing stereotypes in promoting social equality and inclusion.

Source: Fiske, S. T., Cuddy, A. J., Glick, P., and Xu, J. (2002). A model of (often mixed) stereotype content: competence and warmth respectively follow from perceived status and competition. Journal of personality and social psychology, 82(6), 878. DOI: 10.1037%2F0022-3514.82.6.878
Download: A model of (often mixed) stereotype content: competence and warmth respectively follow from perceived status and competition (Table 2, Table 7)

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Social Acceptance: Technology Acceptance Model (TAM)
method.p: Description: The Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) is a widely used theoretical framework that helps researchers and practitioners understand and predict the acceptance and usage of new technologies by individuals. TAM was first introduced in the 1980s by Davis, Bagozzi, and Warshaw and has since been revised several times to incorporate new factors and variables. TAM 3.0 is the latest version of the model, which was developed by Venkatesh and colleagues in 2012. It includes new constructs such as Perceived Enjoyment and Perceived Social Norms and also integrates some of the previous extensions made to the original model. TAM 3.0 is an improvement over previous versions because it provides a more comprehensive and accurate understanding of users' attitudes and intentions towards technology adoption. By using TAM 3.0, researchers and practitioners can better identify the factors that affect user acceptance and tailor their interventions accordingly. This can lead to more successful technology implementations and better outcomes for both individuals and organizations.
Source (TAM): Davis, F., Bagozzi, P. and Warshaw, P. (1989), ‘User acceptance of computer technology - a comparison of two theoretical models’, Management Science 35(8), 982–1003.
Source (TAM3): Venkatesh, V., and Bala, H. (2008). Technology acceptance model 3 and a research agenda on interventions. Decision sciences, 39(2), 273-315.
Download (TAM3): Technology Acceptance Model (p. 313 - 314)

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Social Acceptance: User Acceptance of Information Technology Model (UTAUT)
method.p: Description: Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology (UTAUT) is a model developed through a review of eight prominent models of user acceptance of information technology (IT). UTAUT was empirically validated using data from four organizations and found to outperform the eight individual models in explaining the variance in user intentions to use IT. UTAUT consists of four core determinants of intention and usage, and up to four moderators of key relationships, making it a useful tool for managers to assess the likelihood of success for new technology introductions and design interventions targeted at users who may be less inclined to adopt and use new systems. The paper also makes recommendations for future research.
Source: Venkatesh V, Morris MG, Davis GB, Davis FD. User Acceptance of Information Technology: Toward a Unified View. MIS Quarterly. 2003;27(3):425-478. DOI: 10.2307/30036540

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Motivation: Intrinsic Motivation Inventory (IMI)
method.p: Description: The Intrinsic Motivation Inventory (IMI) is a self-report measure designed to assess an individual's intrinsic motivation, or the internal drive to engage in an activity for its own sake, rather than for external rewards or pressures. The IMI was developed by Richard Ryan and Edward Deci in 1985, and has been widely used in research studies across various domains, including education, sports, and health. Respondents rate each item on a Likert scale, with higher scores indicating greater levels of intrinsic motivation. The IMI has been shown to have good reliability and validity, and has been used in a wide range of research studies examining the effects of various interventions on intrinsic motivation. It is widely regarded as a valuable tool for researchers and practitioners who are interested in understanding and fostering intrinsic motivation.
Source: Deci, E. L., and Ryan, R. M. (2013). Intrinsic motivation and self-determination in human behavior. Springer Science and Business Media.

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Motivation: User Motivation Inventory (UMI)
method.p: Description: The User Motivation Inventory (UMI) is designed to assess the motivation of users to engage with interactive systems. The UMI is an 18-item multidimensional measure rooted in self-determination theory (SDT), and it measures intrinsic motivation, integrated, identified, introjected, and external regulation, as well as amotivation. The UMI has been found to have high reliability and convergent and discriminant validity for each subscale in two studies involving a total of 941 participants. The UMI has been shown to detect differences in motivation for people who consider abandoning a technology compared to those who do not question their use. The abstract concludes by discussing the central role of motivation in users' behavior and experience.

Source: Florian Brühlmann, Beat Vollenwyder, Klaus Opwis, and Elisa D. Mekler. 2018. Measuring the “Why” of Interaction: Development and Validation of the User Motivation Inventory (UMI). In Proceedings of the 2018 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems (CHI '18). Association for Computing Machinery, New York, NY, USA, Paper 106, 1–13. DOI: 10.1145/3173574.3173680

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Mindfulness: Five Facet Mindfulness Questionnaire (FFMQ)
method.p: Description: The Five Facet Mindfulness Questionnaire (FFMQ) is a questionnaire aimed at exploring the mindfulness construct and consists of 39 items that can be answered on a graduated scale (from 1 = "it never or very rarely happens to me" to 5 = "it happens to me often or always"). The FFMQ encompasses five subscales: (1) Non-react, which captures the attitude to avoid reacting to inner experiences. (2) Observe, measuring the ability to observe, notice, and look after sensations, perceptions, thoughts and feelings emerging from one's inner experience. (3) Act with awareness, a measure of the ability to act with focus and absence of distraction. (4) Describe, measuring the ability to describe and label sensations, perceptions, thoughts, and feelings emerging from one's inner experiences in words. (5) Non-judge, measuring the non-judgmental attitude towards one's inner experience.
Source: Baer, R. A., Smith, G. T., Hopkins, J., Krietemeyer, J. and Toney, L. Using self-report assessment methods to explore facets of mindfulness. Assessment 13, 27–45 (2006). PDF

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Emotions: Emotional Regulation Questionnaire (ERQ)
method.p: Description: The Emotional Regulation Questionnaire (ERQ) is a self-report questionnaire that measures an individual's typical strategies for regulating their emotional responses. The ERQ assesses two different strategies: cognitive reappraisal and expressive suppression. Cognitive reappraisal is a strategy that involves changing the way one thinks about a situation in order to regulate emotions. For example, if someone is feeling anxious about a public speaking event, they might try to reframe the situation as an opportunity for personal growth. Expressive suppression is a strategy that involves inhibiting the outward expression of emotions. For example, if someone is feeling sad, they might try to hide their emotions and appear more neutral or positive to others. The ERQ consists of items that describe different scenarios or situations, and respondents rate how frequently they use each strategy to regulate their emotions. The questionnaire has been validated and shown to have good reliability and validity in measuring these two strategies for emotion regulation.

Source: Gouveia, V. V., Moura, H. M. D., Oliveira, I. C. V. D., Ribeiro, M. G. C., Rezende, A. T., and Brito, T. R. D. S. (2018). Emotional Regulation Questionnaire (ERQ): evidence of construct validity and internal consistency. Psico-USF, 23, 461-471. DOI: 10.1590/1413-82712018230306

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Personality: Big Five Inventory (BFI)
method.p: Description: The Big Five Inventory (BFI) was developed by John, Donahue, and Kentle in 1991. The 44-item English BFI was constructed to allow efficient and flexible assessment of the five personality dimensions when there is no need for more differentiated measurement of individual facets. Items were selected from Big Five prototype definitions that had been developed through expert ratings and subsequent factor analytic verification in observer personality ratings. Because single trait adjectives are answered less consistently than when they are accompanied by definitions or elaborations, the BFI does not use single adjectives as items; instead, one or two prototypical trait adjectives served as the item core to which elaborative, clarifying, or contextual information was added. Thus, the BFI items are short and avoid complex sentence structures, retaining the advantages of adjectival items (brevity and simplicity) while avoiding some of their pitfalls (ambiguous or multiple meanings and salient desirability). Participants rate each BFI item on a 5-point scale ranging from 1 (disagree strongly) to 5 (agree strongly); scale scores are computed as the participant's mean item response (i.e., adding all items scored on a scale and dividing by the number of items on the scale). Despite its brevity, the BFI does not sacrifice either content coverage or good psychometric properties. (PsycTests Database Record (c) 2022 APA, all rights reserved)
Source: John, O. P., Donahue, E. M., and Kentle, R. L. (1991). Big Five Inventory (BFI) [Database record]. APA PsycTests. DOI: 10.1037/t07550-000

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Emotions: Emotional State Questionnaire (ESQ)
method.p: Description: The Emotional States Questionnaire (ESQ) is a self-report questionnaire that measures an individual's emotional responses to a specific event or situation. The ESQ is used to assess emotional states such as anxiety, sadness, anger, joy, and other emotions. The questionnaire consists of a list of emotions, and respondents are asked to rate the intensity of each emotion they experienced during a particular event or situation. The ESQ has been used in various research contexts, including psychology, health sciences, and social sciences, to measure emotional responses to different events or interventions. The ESQ has been validated and shown to have good reliability and validity in measuring emotional states. The questionnaire can be modified to suit different research contexts and can be used in both clinical and non-clinical settings to assess emotional responses to specific events or situations.

Source: Cassé-Perrot, C., Fakra, E., Jouve, E., and Blin, O. (2007). Conceptualisation and validation of the" Emotional State Questionnaire (ESQ)": evaluation of an emotional profile. L'encephale, 33(2), 169-178. DOI: 10.1080/10447318.2014.930312

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Emotions: Emotional Metric Outcomes (EMO) Questionnaire
method.p: Description: The Emotional Metric Outcomes (EMO) questionnaire is a new questionnaire designed to assess the emotional outcomes of interaction, especially the interaction of customers with service-provider personnel or software. The EMO is a concise multifactor standardized questionnaire that provides an assessment of transaction-driven personal and relationship emotional outcomes, both positive and negative. The primary purpose of the EMO is to move beyond traditional assessment of satisfaction to achieve a more effective measurement of customers’ emotional responses to products and processes. Psychometric evaluation showed that the EMO and its component scales had high reliability and concurrent validity with loyalty and overall experience metrics in a variety of measurement contexts. Concurrent measurement with the System Usability Scale (SUS) indicated that reported significant correlation of the SUS with likelihood-to-recommend ratings are probably primarily due to emotional rather than utilitarian aspects of the SUS.

Source: Lewis, J. R., and Mayes, D. K. (2014). Development and psychometric evaluation of the emotional metric outcomes (EMO) questionnaire. International Journal of Human-Computer Interaction, 30(9), 685-702. DOI: 10.1080/10447318.2014.930312

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Emotions: Positive and Negative Affect Schedule (PANAS)
method.p: Description: The Positive and Negative Affect Schedule (PANAS) is a self-report questionnaire that consists of two 10-item scales to measure both positive and negative affect. Each item is rated on a 5-point scale of 1 (not at all) to 5 (very much). The measure has been used mainly as a research tool in group studies, but can be utilized within clinical and non-clinical populations as well. Shortened, elongated, and children's versions of the PANAS have been developed, taking approximately 5–10 minutes to complete. Clinical and non-clinical studies have found the PANAS to be a reliable and valid instrument in the assessment of positive and negative affect.

Source: Crawford, John R.; Henry, Julie D. (2004). "The Positive and Negative Affect Schedule (PANAS): Construct validity, measurement properties and normative data in a large non-clinical sample". British Journal of Clinical Psychology. 43 (3): 245–265. DOI: 10.1348/0144665031752934
Link: PANAS Online (German/English), PANAS-SF

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Empathy: Interpersonal Reactivity Index (IRI)
method.p: Description: Describes the Interpersonal Reactivity Index (IRI) and its relationships with measures of social functioning, self-esteem, emotionality, and sensitivity to others. 677 male and 667 female undergraduates served as Ss. Each of the 4 IRI subscales displayed a distinctive and predictable pattern of relationships with these measures, as well as with previous unidimensional empathy measures. Findings provide evidence for a multidimensional approach to empathy. (29 ref) (PsycINFO Database Record (c) 2019 APA, all rights reserved)
Source: Davis, M. H. A multidimensional approach to individual differences in empathy. Cat. Sel. Doc. Psychol. 10, 85 (1980). DOI: 10.1037/0022-3514.44.1.113
Link: IRI Online, Wikipedia

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Self-Esteem: Rosenberg Self-Esteem Scale (RSES)
method.p: Description: The Rosenberg Self-Esteem Scale (RSES) was developed to measure explicit self-esteem. The scale was intended to be mono-dimensional and consists of 10 items containing 5 positive affirmations and 5 negative affirmations. Participants provide a graded response (from 1 = "strongly disagree" to 4 = "strongly agree "). The scale measures one's global self-worth and includes aspects related to self-derogation (i.e., a defence of positive self-images against any threat) and self-enhancement (i.e., rise in the eyes of others).
Source: Rosenberg, M. (1979). Conceiving the self. In Conceiving the self (pp. 318-318). DOI: 10.1016/j.cognition.2007.12.004

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Body-Ownership: Proprioceptive Drift
method.p: Description: Proprioceptive drift refers to the phenomenon in which individuals misperceive the location of their body parts after experiencing a period of sensory stimulation or deprivation. Specifically, proprioceptive drift occurs when an individual perceives their limb as being shifted away from its actual location, in the direction of a visual or tactile stimulus that was applied to the limb. Proprioceptive drift is a multimodal measure combining the processing of visual, tactile, and proprioceptive information and is based on the assumption that there is a reference description of the visual, anatomical and structural properties of the body, and that during the RHI certain multimodal brain regionsact as comparators, matching the (dummy) viewed hand with the body model reference. Modulating this comparator process has an effect on proprioceptive drift, as is evident in various experiments stimulating the brain during the RHI

Source (Original Work): Welch, R.B., Widawski, M.H., Harrington, J. et al. An examination of the relationship between visual capture and prism adaptation. Perception and Psychophysics 25, 126–132 (1979). DOI: 10.3758/BF03198798

Source (Rubber Hand): Tsakiris, M., and Haggard, P. (2005). The Rubber Hand Illusion Revisited: Visuotactile Integration and Self-Attribution. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance, 31(1), 80–91. DOI: 10.1037/0096-1523.31.1.80

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Body-Ownership: Embodiment Questionnaire (EQ)
method.p: Description: The Embodiment Questionnaire (EQ) is a self-report measure developed by Manos Tsakiris and Matthew Longo in 2012 to assess people's sense of embodiment or "body ownership." The questionnaire consists of 33 items that ask participants to rate their agreement with various statements related to their experience of their own body. The EQ assesses three dimensions of embodiment: Ownership: the sense that the body is one's own and under one's control Agency: the sense of initiating and controlling movements of the body Self-location: the sense of where one's body is located in space Each item on the questionnaire is rated on a 7-point Likert scale, with higher scores indicating a stronger sense of embodiment. For example, a sample item from the questionnaire might be: "When I move my hand, I feel that the hand that I see is my own." The EQ has been used in research to study a variety of phenomena related to embodiment, including body image disorders, somatosensory processing, and multisensory integration. The questionnaire is a well-validated tool for assessing people's subjective experience of their own body, which can be useful in understanding the role of embodiment in various psychological processes and disorders.
Source: Longo, M. R., Schüür, F., Kammers, M. P., Tsakiris, M., and Haggard, P. (2008). What is embodiment? A psychometric approach. Cognition, 107(3), 978-998. DOI: 10.1016/j.cognition.2007.12.004

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Body-Ownership: Virtual Embodiment Questionnaire (VEQ)
method.p: Description: The Virtual Embodiment Questionnaire (VEQ) is a self-report measure designed to assess the extent to which individuals experience a sense of ownership and agency over a virtual body in a virtual environment. The VEQ consists of 12 items, which are rated on a 7-point Likert scale from strongly disagree to strongly agree. The questionnaire was developed as a tool to assess the degree of embodiment experienced in virtual reality, which refers to the feeling of being present and fully integrated into a virtual environment. Embodiment in VR is believed to be an important factor in determining the effectiveness of virtual reality experiences, particularly in applications such as virtual therapy, training simulations, and entertainment. The VEQ measures three dimensions of virtual embodiment: ownership, agency, and self-location. Ownership refers to the extent to which an individual feels that a virtual body belongs to them, agency refers to the degree to which they feel in control of the virtual body's movements and actions, and self-location refers to the feeling of being located within the virtual environment.

Source: D. Roth and M. E. Latoschik, "Construction of the Virtual Embodiment Questionnaire (VEQ)," in IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics, vol. 26, no. 12, pp. 3546-3556, Dec. 2020, DOI: 10.1109/TVCG.2020.3023603

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Body-Ownership: Five Statements Post-Questionnaire by Banakou
method.p: Description: To quantify the induced body ownership in virtual reality (VR), Banakou et al. proposes to test hypotheses based on the single-item subscales vrbody ("I felt that the virtual body I saw when looking down at myself was my own body"), mirror ("I felt that the virtual body I saw when looking at myself in the mirror was my own body"), features ("I felt that the virtual body resembled my own real body in terms of shape, skin tone or other visual features"), twobodies ("I felt as if I had two bodies"), and agency ("I felt that the movements of the virtual body were caused by my own movements") on a 7-point Likert scale from -3 (disagree) to +3 (agree).

Source: Banakou, D., Kishore, S., and Slater, M. (2018). Virtually being Einstein results in an improvement in cognitive task performance and a decrease in age bias. Frontiers in psychology, 9, 917. DOI: 10.3389/fpsyg.2018.00917

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Immersion: Immersive Experience Questionnaire for Video (Film IEQ)
method.p: Description: The Immersive Experience Questionnaire (IEQ) is a self-report measure designed to assess the subjective viewers’ experiences of immersive media. Immersion refers to the extent to which an individual feels fully engaged and absorbed in a content. The Film IEQ consists of four subscales: (1) captivation, (2) real-world dissociation, (3) comprehension, and (4) transportation. The Film IEQ allows researchers and practitioners to assess video viewing experiences using a questionnaire that is easy to administer, and that has been empirically validated.

Source: Rigby, J. M., Brumby, D. P., Gould, S. J., and Cox, A. L. (2019, June). Development of a questionnaire to measure immersion in video media: The Film IEQ. In Proceedings of the 2019 ACM International Conference on Interactive Experiences for TV and Online Video (pp. 35-46). DOI: 10.1145/3317697.3323361

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Immersion: Augmented Reality Immersion (ARI)
method.p: Description: The Augmented Reality Immersion (ARI) questionnaire is a self-report measure designed to assess the level of immersion experienced by individuals during an augmented reality (AR) experience. The questionnaire consists of items that ask participants to rate their subjective experience to AR immersion on a Likert scale. The ARI questionnaire was developed as a tool to assess the effectiveness of AR experiences and to help developers and researchers identify areas for improvement in AR design. The questionnaire includes items that assess the sensory, cognitive, and emotional components of ARI, such as the level of engagement, the sense of being physically present in the AR environment, and the feeling of having control over the AR experience. This multi-step process resulted in a 21-item, seven-point Likert-type instrument.

Source: Georgiou, Y., and Kyza, E. A. (2017). The development and validation of the ARI questionnaire: An instrument for measuring immersion in location-based augmented reality settings. International Journal of Human-Computer Studies, 98, 24-37. DOI: 10.1016/j.ijhcs.2016.09.014

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Presence: Witmer-Singer Presence Questionnaire (PQ)
method.p: Description: The Witmer-Singer Presence Questionnaire (PQ) is one of the first self-report measures designed to assess the degree of presence experienced by individuals in virtual environments. Presence is defined as the subjective experience of being in a virtual environment despite physical separation from the real world. The PQ consists of 32 items that are rated on a 7-point Likert scale, with higher scores indicating a greater sense of presence. The items cover a range of factors that contribute to presence, such as the sensory realism of the virtual environment, the level of attention and engagement, and the feeling of control over the virtual environment. The PQ was developed by researchers Thomas Witmer and Janice Singer in 1998 and has since been widely used in virtual reality (VR) research to assess the effectiveness of VR applications and to identify ways to improve the user experience. The questionnaire has been shown to have good psychometric properties, including high reliability and validity, and has been translated into several languages. Overall, the PQ provides a standardized and validated method for measuring presence in virtual environments, which can help researchers and developers better understand and optimize the user experience in VR applications.
Source: Witmer, B. G., and Singer, M. J. (1998). Measuring presence in virtual environments: A presence questionnaire. Presence, 7(3), 225-240. DOI: 10.1162/105474698565686
Download: Witmer-Singer Presence Questionnaire (v 2.0) (p. 49)

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Presence: Immersive Tendencies Questionnaire (ITQ)
method.p: Description: The Immersive Tendencies Questionnaire (ITQ) is a self-report measure designed to assess an individual's tendency to become fully engaged and absorbed in immersive experiences, such as virtual reality (VR), video games, and movies. The ITQ consists of 29 items, which are rated on a 7-point Likert scale ranging from strongly disagree to strongly agree. The ITQ was developed to identify individual differences in the way people engage with and respond to immersive experiences, with the goal of better understanding the psychological and behavioral factors that contribute to immersive tendencies. The questionnaire covers a range of dimensions, including attentional focus, emotional involvement, and absorption in the experience. The ITQ has been shown to have good psychometric properties, including high reliability and validity, and has been used in research to investigate the impact of immersive technologies on cognitive and emotional processes, as well as to identify factors that may influence individual differences in immersive tendencies.
Source: Witmer, B. G., and Singer, M. J. (1998). Measuring presence in virtual environments: A presence questionnaire. Presence, 7(3), 225-240. DOI: 10.1162/105474698565686
Download: Witmer-Singer Immersive Tendencies Questionnaire (v 2.0) (p. 44)

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Presence: iGroup Presence Questionnaire (IPQ)
method.p: Description: The iGroup Presence Questionnaire (IPQ) is a self-report measure designed to assess the subjective experience of presence in a virtual environment, specifically in the context of social interaction. Presence refers to the feeling of being present in a virtual environment as if it were real, and it is considered an important factor in determining the effectiveness of social interactions in virtual reality. It is a 14-item scale for measuring the sense of presence experienced in a virtual environment (VE). It was constructed using a large pool of items and two survey waves with approximately 500 participants. The scale has four subscales: General Presence, Spatial Presence, Involvement, and Experienced Realism.

Source: Regenbrecht, H., and Schubert, T. (2002). Real and illusory interaction enhance presence in virtual environments. Presence: Teleoperators and Virtual Environments, 11(4), 425-434. DOI: 10.1162/105474602760204318

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Usability: Questionnaire for User Interface Satisfaction (QUIS)
method.p: Description: The Questionnaire for User Interface Satisfaction (QUIS) is a self-report measure designed to assess user satisfaction with a computer-based interface. The QUIS was developed by a team of researchers at the University of Maryland in the early 1990s and has been widely used in research and industry to evaluate the usability and user experience of software applications. The QUIS consists of 27 items, which are rated on a 9-point Likert scale. The questionnaire measures user satisfaction across four dimensions: overall reaction, screen, terminology and system feedback, learning factors, and system capabilities.

Source: John P. Chin, Virginia A. Diehl, and Kent L. Norman. 1988. Development of an instrument measuring user satisfaction of the human-computer interface. In Proceedings of the SIGCHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems (CHI '88). Association for Computing Machinery, New York, NY, USA, 213–218. DOI: 10.1145/57167.57203

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Usability: Pragmatic and Hedonic Qualities (AttrakDiff/AttrakDiff Mini)
method.p: Description: The AttrakDiff questionnaire is a self-report measure designed to assess the perceived hedonic and pragmatic quality of a product, service, or system. The questionnaire was developed by Marc Hassenzahl and colleagues in 2003 and has been widely used in research and industry to evaluate the user experience of various products and systems. The AttrakDiff questionnaire consists of 28 bipolar items, which are rated on a 7-point Likert scale from "not at all" to "very much." The questionnaire measures the perceived hedonic quality (attractiveness, stimulation, and identification) and pragmatic quality (usability, clarity, and efficiency) of the product, service, or system. The perceived hedonic quality of a product refers to its emotional appeal, aesthetics, and the degree to which it satisfies the user's personal preferences and values. The perceived pragmatic quality, on the other hand, refers to its functional aspects, including ease of use, efficiency, and effectiveness. The AttrakDiff questionnaire provides a standardized and validated method for assessing the perceived quality of a product, service, or system, which can help researchers and developers better understand and optimize the user experience. By measuring both the hedonic and pragmatic aspects of the user experience, the AttrakDiff questionnaire can provide a more comprehensive understanding of the factors that influence user satisfaction and acceptance of a product or system.

Source: Hassenzahl, M., Burmester, M., and Koller, F. (2003). AttrakDiff: Ein Fragebogen zur Messung wahrgenommener hedonischer und pragmatischer Qualität. Mensch und Computer 2003: Interaktion in Bewegung, 187-196.

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Usability: User Experience Questionnaire (UEQ)
method.p: Description: The User Experience Questionnaire (UEQ) is a self-report measure designed to assess the overall user experience of a product or system. Usually 3 to 5 minutes are sufficient for a participant to read the instructions and complete the questionnaire. The questionnaire was developed in 2008 and has been widely used in research and industry to evaluate the usability and user experience of various products and systems. The UEQ consists of 26 items, which are rated on a 7-point Likert scale. The questionnaire measures six dimensions of the user experience: attractiveness, perspicuity, efficiency, dependability, stimulation, and novelty. The short version, UEQ-S, only supports the scales hedonic quality and pragmatic quality. The UEQ is currently available in 17 languages (German, English, French, Italian, Russian, Spanish, Portuguese, Turkish, Chinese, Japanese, Indonesian, Dutch, Estonian, Slovene, Swedish, Greek, and Polish). The UEQ can be used either in paper-and-pencil form or as an online questionnaire.

Source: Laugwitz, B., Schrepp, M. and Held, T. (2008). Construction and evaluation of a user experience questionnaire. In: Holzinger, A. (Ed.): USAB 2008, LNCS 5298, pp. 63-76. DOI: 10.1007/978-3-540-89350-9_6

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Usability: Usability Metric for User Experience (UMUX)
method.p: Description: The Usability Metric for User Experience (UMUX) is a four-item scale used for the subjective assessment of an application’s perceived usability. It is designed to provide results similar to those obtained with the 10-item System Usability Scale, and is organized around the ISO 9241-11 definition of usability. A pilot version was assembled from candidate items, which was then tested alongside the System Usability Scale during usability testing. It was shown that the two scales correlate well, are reliable, and both align on one underlying usability factor. In addition, the Usability Metric for User Experience is compact enough to serve as a usability module in a broader user experience metric.
Source: Finstad, K. (2010). The usability metric for user experience. Interacting with computers, 22(5), 323-327. DOI: 10.1016/j.intcom.2010.04.004

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Usability: System Usability Scale (SUS)
method.p: Description: The System Usability Scale (SUS) provides a "quick and dirty" but reliable tool for measuring usability. It consists of a 10-item questionnaire with five response options ranging from Strongly agree to Strongly disagree. Originally created by John Brooke in 1986, it allows you to evaluate a wide variety of products and services, including hardware, software, mobile devices, websites, and applications.

Source: Brooke, J. (1996). "SUS: a "quick and dirty" usability scale". In P. W. Jordan; B. Thomas; B. A. Weerdmeester; A. L. McClelland (eds.). Usability Evaluation in Industry. London: Taylor and Francis.
Download: SUS Score Calculator, Download, Download with Instructions

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Cognitive Load: NASA Task Load Index (NASA-TLX)
method.p: Description: The NASA Task Load Index (NASA-TLX) is a widely used, subjective, multidimensional assessment tool that rates perceived workload in order to assess a task, system, or team's effectiveness or other aspects of performance. There is a weighted (TLX) and raw version (RTLX) assessing mental demand, physical demand, temporal demand, performance, effort, and frustration.

Source: Hart, Sandra G.; Staveland, Lowell E. (1988). Development of NASA-TLX (Task Load Index): Results of Empirical and Theoretical Research. In Hancock, Peter A.; Meshkati, Najmedin (eds.). Human Mental Workload. Advances in Psychology. Vol. 52. Amsterdam: North Holland. pp. 139–183. DOI:10.1016/S0166-4115(08)62386-9.

Follow-Up Source: Hart, Sandra G. (October 2006). NASA-task load index (NASA-TLX); 20 years later. In Proceedings of the human factors and ergonomics society annual meeting (Vol. 50, No. 9, pp. 904-908). Sage CA: Los Angeles, CA: Sage publications. DOI:10.1016/S0166-4115(08)62386-9.
Downloads: PDF, Online Version, Apps for iOS and Android

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Gaming Experience: Game Experience Questionnaire (GEQ)
method.p: Description: The Game Experience Questionnaire has a modular structure and consists of: 1. the core questionnaire, 2. the Social Presence Module, and 3. the Post-game Module. In addition to these modules, a concise in-game version of the GEQ was developed. All three modules are meant to be administered immediately after the game session has finished, in the order given above. Parts one and two probe the players' feelings and thoughts while playing the game; part three, the post-game module, assesses how players felt after they had stopped playing.
Source: IJsselsteijn, W. A., de Kort, Y. A. W., and Poels, K. (2013). The Game Experience Questionnaire. Technische Universiteit Eindhoven. PDF

Source: Law, E. L. C., Brühlmann, F., and Mekler, E. D. (2018, October). Systematic review and validation of the game experience questionnaire (geq)-implications for citation and reporting practice. In Proceedings of the 2018 Annual Symposium on Computer-Human Interaction in Play (pp. 257-270). 10.1145/3242671.3242683

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Gaming Experience: Game User Experience Satisfaction Scale (GUESS)
method.p: Description: The Game User Experience Satisfaction Scale (GUESS) is a validated instrument designed to assess video game satisfaction across multiple dimensions crucial to the gaming experience. Developed to address the inadequacies of existing evaluation tools in capturing comprehensive feedback during playtesting and game evaluation, GUESS employs a psychometrically robust framework based on extensive research and methodology best practices. It consists of nine subscales including aspects like usability, playability, narratives, play engrossment, enjoyment, creative freedom, audio and visual aesthetics, personal gratification, and social connectivity. These factors collectively measure a game's ability to deliver a satisfying user experience, making GUESS applicable across various genres and platforms in the gaming industry. The scale's development involved a mixed-method approach, including item pool generation, expert review, pilot testing, exploratory and confirmatory factor analysis, ensuring its reliability and validity in assessing the multidimensional construct of game satisfaction.

Source: Phan, M., Keebler, J., and Chaparro, B. (2016). The Development and Validation of the Game User Experience Satisfaction Scale (GUESS). Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 58. doi: 10.1177/0018720816669646

Source: Joseph R. Keebler, William J. Shelstad, Dustin C. Smith, Barbara S. Chaparro, and Mikki H. Phan. 2020. Validation of the GUESS-18: A Short Version of the Game User Experience Satisfaction Scale (GUESS). J. Usability Studies 16, 1 (nov 2020), 49–62.

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Gaming Experience: The Player Experience Inventory (PXI, miniPXI)
method.p: Description: The Player Experience Inventory (PXI) is a validated, theoretically grounded tool designed for Games User Research that measures both immediate functional consequences and broader psychosocial emotional responses to provide actionable insights into how specific design choices shape player experience.

Source: Vanden Abeele, V., Spiel, K., Nacke, L., Johnson, D., Gerling, K. (2020). Development and validation of the player experience inventory: A scale to measure player experiences at the level of functional and psychosocial consequences. International Journal of Human-Computer Studies, 135, 102370. doi: 10.1016/j.ijhcs.2019.102370

Source: Aqeel Haider, Casper Harteveld, Daniel Johnson, Max V. Birk, Regan L. Mandryk, Magy Seif El-Nasr, Lennart E. Nacke, Kathrin Gerling, and Vero Vanden Abeele. 2022. MiniPXI: Development and Validation of an Eleven-Item Measure of the Player Experience Inventory. Proc. ACM Hum.-Comput. Interact. 6, CHI PLAY, Article 244 (October 2022), doi: 10.1145/3549507

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Gaming Experience: Gamification User Types Hexad Scale
method.p: Description: The Hexad model is a framework used in gamification to align game design elements with user personality traits, aiming to personalize user experiences more effectively. It categorizes users into six types based on their motivations and interactions with gamified systems. These types are Achievers, Philanthropists, Socializers, Free Spirits, Players, and Disruptors. Each type responds differently to gamified elements, like challenges or social engagement features. Recognizing the challenge of directly assessing user preferences within these categories, researchers developed a 24-item Hexad scale to measure individual inclination towards these six motivations. This scale has undergone various validations, including internal consistency and factor analysis, to ensure its reliability and accuracy. It also correlates these user types with the Big Five personality traits, providing a robust tool for understanding user behavior in gamified systems. However, concerns about the length of the 24-item questionnaire potentially leading to participant dropout and fatigue prompted the creation of a condensed 12-item version of the Hexad scale. This shorter version retains the integrity of the original while enhancing usability and reducing participant burden. It has been validated through factor analyses and shows improved model fit and reliability. This makes the Hexad-12 an effective and efficient tool for deploying tailored gamification strategies in UX design, particularly when included in larger surveys where brevity and participant engagement are crucial.

Source: Tondello, G. F., Wehbe, R. R., Diamond, L., Busch, M., Marczewski, A., and Nacke, L. E. (2016). The Gamification User Types Hexad Scale. CHI PLAY '16: Proceedings of the 2016 Annual Symposium on Computer-Human Interaction in Play. Association for Computing Machinery. doi: 10.1145/2967934.2968082

Source: Jeanine Krath, Maximilian Altmeyer, Gustavo F. Tondello, and Lennart E. Nacke. 2023. Hexad-12: Developing and Validating a Short Version of the Gamification User Types Hexad Scale. In Proceedings of the 2023 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems. Association for Computing Machinery, New York, NY, USA, Article 677, 18 pages. 10.1145/3544548.3580968

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Gaming Experience: Player Experience of Need Satisfaction (PENS)
method.p: Description: The Player Experience of Need Satisfaction (PENS) is a questionnaire grounded in Self-Determination Theory for assessing how strongly a game supports psychological needs such as competence, autonomy, and relatedness. In games user research, it is useful when the goal is to understand motivational quality, need satisfaction, and the experiential foundations of player engagement.

Source: Johnson, D., Gardner, M. J., and Perry, R. (2018). Validation of two game experience scales: The Player Experience of Need Satisfaction (PENS) and Game Experience Questionnaire (GEQ). Int. J. Hum. Comput. Stud., 118, 38–46. doi: 10.1007/s11257-019-09223-w
Source: Ryan, R. M., Rigby, C. S., and Przybylski, A. (2006). The Motivational Pull of Video Games: A Self-Determination Theory Approach. Motiv. Emot., 30(4), 344–360. doi: 10.1007/s11031-006-9051-8

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Gaming Experience: The Video Game Dispositional Flow Scale (VGDFS)
method.p: Description: The Video Game Dispositional Flow Scale (VGDFS) is a 28-item psychometric instrument developed and validated through a multi-phase study to accurately measure the dispositional flow experience of adult players by utilizing a hierarchical antecedent model that addresses previous conceptual and empirical flaws.

Source: Cai, X., Cebollada, J., Cortiñas, M. (2022). Self-report measure of dispositional flow experience in the video game context: Conceptualisation and scale development. International Journal of Human-Computer Studies, 159, 102746. doi: 10.1016/j.ijhcs.2021.102746

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Gaming Experience: The Gameful Experience Questionnaire (GAMEFULQUEST)
method.p: Description: GAMEFULQUEST is a validated, multi-dimensional instrument developed to measure a user's gameful experience within gamified systems and services, providing a standardized way to evaluate how effectively a design evokes game-like motivations and behaviors for user-adapted gamification.

Source: Högberg, J., Hamari, J., Wästlund, E. (2019). Gameful Experience Questionnaire (GAMEFULQUEST): An instrument for measuring the perceived gamefulness of system use. User Modeling and User-Adapted Interaction, 29(3), 619-660. doi: 10.1007/s11257-019-09223-w

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Gaming Experience: Game Engagement Questionnaire (Engagement GEQ)
method.p: Description: The Game Engagement Questionnaire (GEQ) is a psychometrically validated tool developed using classical and Rasch analyses to measure the subjective levels of deep engagement in video game-playing, specifically to explore its role as a determinant of the impact of violent video games.

Source: Brockmyer, J. H., Fox, C. M., Curtiss, K. A., McBroom, E., Burkhart, K. M., Pidruzny, J. N. (2009). The development of the Game Engagement Questionnaire: A measure of engagement in video game-playing. Journal of experimental social psychology, 45(4), 624-634. doi: 10.1016/j.jesp.2009.02.016

method.title: Quantitative Objective Performance: Input Error Rate (%)
method.p: Description: Input error rate is a measure of the frequency with which a user makes errors while interacting with a computer system or interface. It is a commonly used metric in human-computer interaction (HCI) research and usability testing, as errors can have a significant impact on user performance, efficiency, and satisfaction. Input error rate can be measured in a variety of ways, depending on the specific task and interface being evaluated. For example, it may be measured as the percentage of keystrokes that result in an incorrect character being entered, or the percentage of clicks or taps that are made in the wrong location on a screen. Input error rate can be affected by a range of factors, including the complexity of the task, the design and layout of the interface, the user's level of experience and familiarity with the system, and the type and sensitivity of the input device being used. By tracking input error rate and analyzing any patterns or trends that emerge, designers and researchers can identify areas where users may be encountering difficulties or confusion, and make informed decisions about how to improve interface designs and optimize task performance.

method.title: Quantitative Objective Performance: Pointing Accuracy/Precision
method.p: Description: Pointing accuracy and pointing precision are two related but distinct measures of how accurately a user can point to a target on a computer screen using a pointing device such as a mouse or touchpad. Pointing accuracy, also known as target offset, is a measure of how close the user's pointing device clicks or taps are to the center of the target. Target offset is typically calculated by measuring the distance between the center of the target and the location where the user actually clicked or tapped. Pointing precision, on the other hand, is a measure of how consistently the user can point to the same location on the screen over multiple attempts. It is calculated by measuring the variance or standard deviation of the user's clicks or taps relative to the center of the target. Both pointing accuracy and precision are important usability metrics for evaluating the effectiveness of pointing devices and interface designs. A pointing device with high accuracy but low precision may be frustrating to use, as the user is unable to consistently click on the same location. Conversely, a pointing device with high precision but low accuracy may also be frustrating, as the user may have to repeatedly adjust their aim to hit the target. In HCI research, pointing accuracy and precision are often used in conjunction with other metrics such as task completion time and user satisfaction to provide a comprehensive evaluation of interface design and usability.

method.title: Quantitative Objective Performance: Text Input Speed in Words per Minute (WPM)
method.p: Description: Text input speed in words per minute (WPM) is a measure of how quickly a person can type text on a keyboard or other input device. It is a commonly used metric to assess a person's typing proficiency and speed. To measure text input speed, a person is typically given a text passage to type, and their speed is measured by the number of words they can accurately type within a set time period, usually one minute. For example, if a person types 80 words correctly in one minute, their text input speed would be 80 WPM. Text input speed can be affected by a range of factors, including typing technique, keyboard layout, familiarity with the text passage, and level of experience with typing. In the context of human-computer interaction, text input speed is an important consideration for designing interfaces and evaluating the usability of input devices. In evaluations of text entry methods, participants enter phrases of text using a technique of interest while performance data are collected. A phrase set by MacKenzie and Soukoreff describes and publishes a collection of 500 phrases for such evaluations. Utility programs are also provided to compute statistical properties of the phrase set, or any other phrase set. The merits of using a pre-defined phrase set are described as are methodological considerations, such as attaining results that are generalizable and the possible addition of punctuation and other characters

Source: I. Scott MacKenzie and R. William Soukoreff. 2003. Phrase sets for evaluating text entry techniques. In CHI '03 Extended Abstracts on Human Factors in Computing Systems (CHI EA '03). Association for Computing Machinery, New York, NY, USA, 754–755. https://doi.org/10.1145/765891.765971

p.a: Phrase Sets for Evaluating Text Entry Techniques

method.title: Quantitative Objective Text Metric: Sentiment Analysis
method.p: Description: Sentiment analysis, also known as opinion mining, is a field of study that analyzes people's opinions, sentiments, evaluations, attitudes, and emotions from written language. It is a prevalent tool in the processing of natural language, helping to ascertain the contextual polarity of textual material, whether the expressed opinion in documents, tweets, and other social media is positive, negative, or neutral. Applications of sentiment analysis are broad and highly useful in business environments. Companies use it to understand consumer sentiment toward products, brand reputation, and service quality by analyzing data from social media, survey responses, or online reviews. The technique often involves machine learning algorithms or lexicon-based approaches to classify the sentiment of texts. In machine learning-based approaches, text data is usually converted into a format that can be fed into algorithms (like support vector machines, neural networks, or deep learning models) that classify the sentiment. The model learns from a pre-labeled dataset in a supervised learning setting to recognize sentiment in new inputs. In contrast, lexicon-based approaches involve calculating sentiment scores based on the presence of predefined words associated with positive or negative sentiments.
Source: Liu, B. (2012). Sentiment Analysis and Opinion Mining. Morgan and Claypool Publishers

method.title: Quantitative Objective Text Metric: Lexical Diversity Analysis
method.p: Description: Lexical diversity measures the range of different words used in a text, providing insights into the vocabulary richness and usage variety within a document. It is often quantified using metrics such as the Type-Token Ratio (TTR), which calculates the ratio of unique words (types) to the total number of words (tokens) in a text. This analysis helps in assessing the linguistic complexity and the style of writing, important in educational settings, authorship attribution, and language studies.
Source: McCarthy, P. M., and Jarvis, S. (2010). MTLD, vocd-D, and HD-D: A validation study of sophisticated approaches to lexical diversity assessment. Behavior Research Methods, 42(2), 381-392.

method.title: Quantitative Objective Text Metric: Coherence Analysis
method.p: Description: Text coherence refers to the logical and semantic connection between different parts of a text, which makes the content easy to understand and follow. Coherence is assessed through the use of various linguistic devices like transitional phrases, pronoun reference, and theme development. Analyzing coherence is crucial in educational evaluations, automated essay scoring, and any application where the flow of text impacts its comprehensibility and effectiveness.

Source: Graesser, A. C., McNamara, D. S., Louwerse, M. M., and Cai, Z. (2004). Coh-Metrix: Analysis of text on cohesion and language. Behavioral Research Methods, Instruments, and Computers, 36(2), 193-202.

method.title: Quantitative Objective Text Metric: Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA)
method.p: Description: Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) is a generative statistical model that allows sets of observations to be explained by unobserved groups, helping to understand why some parts of the data are similar. In text analysis, LDA is used to classify text in a document to a particular topic, enabling the discovery of topic distribution across a corpus. This technique is widely used in digital humanities, content analysis, and information retrieval to enhance the accuracy of document classification.
Source: Blei, D. M., Ng, A. Y., and Jordan, M. I. (2003). Latent dirichlet allocation. The Journal of Machine Learning Research, 3, 993-1022.

method.title: Quantitative Objective Text Metric: Readability Scores
method.p: Description: Readability scores assess the ease with which a reader can understand a written text. The Flesch-Kincaid Reading Ease, Gunning Fog Index, and SMOG Index are common formulas used to estimate the readability. These scores consider factors like sentence length and word difficulty to provide a grade level necessary to comprehend the text. Readability is crucial in educational content development, legal documents, and healthcare communications to ensure accessibility and comprehensibility.

Source: Kincaid, J. P., Fishburne, R. P., Rogers, R. L., and Chissom, B. S. (1975). Derivation of new readability formulas (Automated Readability Index, Fog Count and Flesch Reading Ease Formula) for Navy enlisted personnel. Research Branch Report 8-75, Naval Technical Training, U. S. Naval Air Station, Memphis, TN.

method.title: Quantitative Objective Text Metric: Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF)
method.p: Description: Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF) is a statistical measure used to evaluate how important a word is to a document in a collection or corpus. The importance increases proportionally to the number of times a word appears in the document but is offset by the frequency of the word in the corpus. TF-IDF is often used in text mining and information retrieval to weight terms during document retrieval and indexing.
Source: Salton, G., and McGill, M. J. (1983). Introduction to Modern Information Retrieval. McGraw-Hill, Inc.

method.title: Quantitative Objective Text Metric: Frequency Counts
method.p: Description: Frequency counts involve tallying the occurrences of words, phrases, or characters in a text. This simple yet powerful analytical tool is essential for identifying the most frequently used terms within a text, helping in keyword analysis, thematic studies, and understanding linguistic habits. Frequency counts are fundamental in computational linguistics, content analysis, and natural language processing.
Source: Manning, C. D., and Schütze, H. (1999). Foundations of Statistical Natural Language Processing. MIT Press.

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Satisfaction: Net Promoter Score (NPS)
method.p: Description: The Net Promoter Score is calculated based on responses to a single question: "On a scale from 0 to 10, how likely are you to recommend our company/product/service/system/prototype/method to a friend or colleague?" Based on their ratings, respondents are categorized into three segments:
1. Promoters (score 9-10): These are highly satisfied customers who are likely to engage in positive word-of-mouth activities, including recommending the product or service to others.
2. Passives (score 7-8): Satisfied but unenthusiastic customers who are vulnerable to competitive offerings.
3. Detractors (score 0-6): Unsatisfied customers who could potentially damage the brand and impede growth through negative word-of-mouth.

The NPS is derived by subtracting the percentage of Detractors from the percentage of Promoters. The score itself can range from -100 (if every customer is a Detractor) to 100 (if every customer is a Promoter). NPS is revered for its simplicity and its strong correlation with a company's growth and customer loyalty levels. A higher NPS indicates more Promoters relative to Detractors, which often correlates with better overall business performance. Companies utilize NPS to identify strengths and weaknesses in customer service and product offerings and to motivate an organization towards customer-centric improvement. However, NPS has its critics who argue that it oversimplifies customer experiences by boiling them down to a single metric, potentially ignoring other important aspects of customer satisfaction and loyalty. Furthermore, NPS does not differentiate between the passivity of different demographic groups or market segments, which could lead to misinterpretations.
Source:Frederick F. Reichheld: The number one you need to grow. In: Harvard Business Review

method.title: Quantitative Objective Performance: Fitts' Law (Throughput)
method.p: Description: Fitts' law states that the amount of time required for a person to move a pointer (e.g., mouse cursor) to a target area is a function of the distance to the target divided by the size of the target. Thus, the longer the distance and the smaller the target's size, the longer it takes. According to Fitts' law, human movement can be modeled by analogy to the transmission of information denoted as throughput in bits/s. Fitts' popular model has been widely adopted in numerous research areas, including kinematics, human factors, and human-computer interaction (HCI).

1D Fitts' law task: MacKenzie, I. S. (1992). Fitts' law as a research and design tool in human-computer interaction. Human-computer interaction, 7(1), 91-139. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1207/s15327051hci0701_3

2D Fitts' law task: MacKenzie, I. S., and Buxton, W. (1992, June). Extending Fitts' law to two-dimensional tasks. In Proceedings of the SIGCHI conference on Human factors in computing systems (pp. 219-226). https://doi.org/10.1145/142750.142794

3D Fitts' law task: Cha, Y., and Myung, R. (2013). Extended Fitts' law for 3D pointing tasks using 3D target arrangements. International Journal of Industrial Ergonomics, 43(4), 350-355. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ergon.2013.05.005

method.title: Quantitative Objective Memory: Stroop Test
method.p: Description: The Stroop test is a classic neuropsychological task that is widely used to assess memory attention, cognitive flexibility, and processing speed. The test was developed by J.R. Stroop in 1935 and has been used in research and clinical settings to evaluate executive functioning, brain injury, and various cognitive disorders. In the standard version of the Stroop test, participants are presented with a list of words that are printed in colored ink. The task is to name the color of the ink as quickly and accurately as possible. The test consists of three parts: In the first part, the words are all printed in black ink, and participants are asked to read the words as quickly as possible. In the second part, the words are printed in different colors, but the color of the ink and the word meaning are the same. For example, the word "red" might be printed in red ink. Participants are again asked to name the color of the ink. In the third part, the words are printed in different colors, and the color of the ink and the word meaning are different. For example, the word "red" might be printed in blue ink. Participants are asked to name the color of the ink as quickly and accurately as possible. The Stroop effect occurs when the word meaning interferes with the naming of the ink color, resulting in slower reaction times and increased errors in the third part of the test. This interference is thought to reflect the automatic processing of word meanings and the need for cognitive control to overcome this interference and focus on the task-relevant information (i.e., the color of the ink). The Stroop test is a widely used and well-established measure of attentional control and cognitive flexibility. It is used in both research and clinical settings to assess various cognitive disorders such as attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), Alzheimer's disease, and traumatic brain injury.
Source: Kirchner, W. K. (1958). Age differences in short-term retention of rapidly changing information. Journal of Experimental Psychology, 55(4), 352–358. 10.1207/s15327051hci0701_3
Link Stroop-Test Online, Wikipedia

method.title: Quantitative Objective Memory: N-back Task
method.p: Description: The n-back task is a widely used cognitive task that measures working memory capacity and cognitive control. The task requires participants to monitor a series of stimuli (usually visual or auditory) and indicate whether the current stimulus matches the stimulus presented n items back in the series. For example, in a 2-back task, participants would be presented with a sequence of letters or numbers and would have to indicate whether the current letter or number matches the one presented two items back in the sequence. In a 3-back task, participants would have to match the current stimulus to the one presented three items back, and so on. The n-back task is a relatively simple task that can be easily administered and scored, making it a popular tool in cognitive research. The task is also sensitive to individual differences in working memory capacity and cognitive control, and has been used in studies investigating various cognitive disorders such as schizophrenia, depression, and ADHD. The n-back task can be adapted to different modalities, such as auditory and visual, and can also be modified by changing the value of n to vary the level of difficulty. A more difficult n-back task, such as a 3-back or 4-back task, requires greater working memory capacity and cognitive control to perform accurately, while an easier task such as a 1-back task would be less demanding.
Source: Kirchner, W. K. (1958). Age differences in short-term retention of rapidly changing information. Journal of Experimental Psychology, 55(4), 352–358. 10.1037/h0043688
Link N-Back Task Online, Wikipedia

method.title: Quantitative Objective Performance: Task Completion Time (TCT)
method.p: Description: In the context of human-computer interaction (HCI), task completion time (TCT) refers to the amount of time it takes for a user to complete a specific task or set of tasks using an interface or system. TCT is an important usability metric that can be used to evaluate the efficiency and effectiveness of an interface design, as well as to compare different interface designs. TCT can be measured in a variety of ways, depending on the specific task and interface being evaluated. For example, TCT for a simple task like filling out a form might be measured as the time it takes for a user to complete all required fields and submit the form. For a more complex task like creating a digital illustration, TCT might be measured as the time it takes for a user to complete all steps in the process, from initial sketching to final rendering. In addition to providing insight into the overall efficiency of an interface design, TCT can also help identify specific areas where users may encounter difficulties or confusion during a task. By tracking TCT and analyzing any patterns or trends that emerge, designers and researchers can make informed decisions about how to improve interface designs and optimize task performance.

method.title: Quantitative Objective Bias: Implicit Association Test (IAT)
method.p: Description: The Implicit Association Test (IAT) is a psychological tool that measures implicit biases or attitudes towards certain social groups, concepts, or topics. The test was first developed by social psychologists Anthony Greenwald, Debbie McGhee, and Jordan Schwartz in the 1990s. The IAT measures the strength of automatic associations between mental representations of certain social groups, concepts, or topics and evaluations (e.g., positive or negative). The test involves a computer-based task in which participants categorize stimuli (e.g., words or images) into one of two categories as quickly as possible. For example, participants might categorize words as "good" or "bad" and images of different social groups (e.g., white and black faces) as belonging to one of two categories (e.g., "American" or "foreign"). The IAT measures the relative speed with which participants categorize stimuli when the categories are paired in different ways, providing an indirect measure of implicit biases or attitudes towards the categories being tested. If participants are faster to categorize "good" words with "white" faces and "bad" words with "black" faces than the reverse, this suggests an implicit bias towards white faces and against black faces. The IAT has been used in research to study various topics, including racial bias, gender bias, and attitudes towards different social groups. The IAT has been criticized for its reliance on a single task to measure implicit attitudes, and some researchers have called for more diverse measures of implicit attitudes. Despite these criticisms, the IAT remains a widely used and influential tool in social psychology research.

Source: Nosek, B. A., Greenwald, A. G., Banaji, M. R. (2005). Understanding and using the Implicit Association Test: II. Method variables and construct validity. Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin. 31 (2): 166–180. DOI: 10.1177/0146167204271418
Link IAT Testing Online, Wikipedia

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Self-Efficacy: Cybersecurity Self-Efficacy in Smart Homes (CySESH) Scale
method.p: Description: The Cybersecurity Self-Efficacy in Smart Homes (CySESH) scale is a 12-item unidimensional measure of domain-specific self-efficacy beliefs. CySESH showed the expected relationship trends with other established psychological traits (i.e., self-efficacy in information security, outcome expectation, self-esteem, and optimism), which confirmed its distinctness. By following state-of-the-art test construction and open science processes, the scale provides researchers and practitioners with a meaningful evaluation tool.

Source: Nele Borgert, Oliver D. Reithmaier, Luisa Jansen, Larina Hillemann, Ian Hussey, and Malte Elson. 2023. Home Is Where the Smart Is: Development and Validation of the Cybersecurity Self-Efficacy in Smart Homes (CySESH) Scale. In Proceedings of the 2023 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems (CHI '23). Association for Computing Machinery, New York, NY, USA, Article 507, 1–15. DOI: 10.1145/3544548.3580860

method.title: Quantitative Subjective User Experience: meCUE Questionnaire (2.0)
method.p: Description: The meCUE 2.0 is a modular self-report questionnaire for evaluating user experience. It covers instrumental product perceptions, non-instrumental perceptions, emotions, consequences of use, and an overall evaluation. It is suitable when researchers want a broader UX assessment beyond classical usability-only instruments.

Source: Minge, M., and Thüring, M. (2018). The MeCUE Questionnaire (2.0): Meeting Five Basic Requirements for Lean and Standardized UX Assessment. In Proceedings of the Mensch und Computer 2018 Conference. DOI: 10.1007/978-3-319-91797-9_33

method.title: Quantitative Subjective User Experience: User Engagement Scale (UES)
method.p: Description: The User Engagement Scale measures the degree to which users are focused, involved, and absorbed during interaction. It is useful when engagement should be distinguished from general usability or overall satisfaction.

Source (Original Concept): O'Brien, H. L., and Toms, E. G. (2010). The development and evaluation of a survey to measure user engagement. Journal of the American Society for Information Science and Technology, 61(1), 50-69. DOI: 10.1002/asi.21229

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Emotions: Self-Assessment Manikin (SAM)
method.p: Description: The Self-Assessment Manikin (SAM) is a non-verbal pictorial instrument for measuring affective responses on the dimensions valence, arousal, and dominance. It is especially useful when a compact and language-light emotion measure is needed.

Source: Bradley, M. M., and Lang, P. J. (1994). Measuring emotion: The self-assessment manikin and the semantic differential. Journal of Behavior Therapy and Experimental Psychiatry, 25(1), 49-59. DOI: 10.1016/0005-7916(94)90063-9

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Flow: Flow State Scale (FSS)
method.p: Description: The Flow State Scale (FSS) is a self-report measure for assessing the subjective experience of flow during an activity. It captures aspects such as concentration, sense of control, merging of action and awareness, autotelic experience, and intrinsic enjoyment. In HCI, it is useful for studying highly engaging interactions such as games, creative tools, immersive environments, and performance-oriented interfaces.

Source: Jackson, S. A., and Marsh, H. W. (1996). Development and validation of a scale to measure optimal experience: The Flow State Scale. Journal of Sport and Exercise Psychology, 18(1), 17-35. DOI: 10.1123/jsep.18.1.17

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Health: Simulator Sickness Questionnaire (SSQ)
method.p: Description: The Simulator Sickness Questionnaire is one of the standard self-report measures for symptoms such as nausea, oculomotor discomfort, and disorientation after simulator or virtual environment exposure. It is widely used in VR and XR studies to assess cybersickness-related symptoms.

Source: Kennedy, R. S., Lane, N. E., Berbaum, K. S., and Lilienthal, M. G. (1993). Simulator Sickness Questionnaire: An Enhanced Method for Quantifying Simulator Sickness. The International Journal of Aviation Psychology, 3(3), 203-220. DOI: 10.1207/s15327108ijap0303_3

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Health: Virtual Reality Sickness Questionnaire (VRSQ)
method.p: Description: The Virtual Reality Sickness Questionnaire is a VR-focused self-report instrument for assessing sickness symptoms in head-mounted display environments. It is often used as a more targeted alternative to the SSQ in modern VR studies.

Source: Kim, H. K., Park, J., Choi, Y., and Choe, M. (2018). Virtual reality sickness questionnaire (VRSQ): Motion sickness measurement index in a virtual reality environment. Applied Ergonomics, 69, 66-73. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2017.12.016

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Human-Likeness: Godspeed Questionnaire Series
method.p: Description: The Godspeed Questionnaire Series is widely used in HRI to assess perceptions of robots and artificial agents. It contains scales for anthropomorphism, animacy, likeability, perceived intelligence, and perceived safety, and is suitable for studies comparing robot or agent designs.

Source: Bartneck, C., Kulić, D., Croft, E., and Zoghbi, S. (2009). Measurement Instruments for the Anthropomorphism, Animacy, Likeability, Perceived Intelligence, and Perceived Safety of Robots. International Journal of Social Robotics, 1, 71-81. DOI: 10.1007/s12369-008-0001-3

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Social Acceptance: Robotic Social Attributes Scale (RoSAS)
method.p: Description: The Robotic Social Attributes Scale measures social perceptions of robots across dimensions such as warmth, competence, and discomfort. It is useful when evaluating how acceptable or socially appropriate a robot appears to users.

Source: Carpinella, C. M., Wyman, A. B., Perez, M. A., and Stroessner, S. J. (2017). The Robotic Social Attributes Scale (RoSAS): Development and Validation. In Proceedings of the 2017 ACM/IEEE International Conference on Human-Robot Interaction. DOI: 10.1145/2909824.3020208

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Trust: Trust in Automation Questionnaire (TiA)
method.p: Description: The Trust in Automation Questionnaire is used to assess a person's trust in automated systems. It is relevant for HCI studies involving intelligent systems, decision support, assistance, or semi-autonomous technologies.

Source: Körber, M. (2018). Theoretical Considerations and Development of a Questionnaire to Measure Trust in Automation. In Proceedings of the 20th Congress of the International Ergonomics Association (IEA 2018). DOI: 10.1007/978-3-319-96074-6_2

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Social Acceptance: Almere Model Questionnaire
method.p: Description: The Almere Model Questionnaire is an acceptance instrument for assistive social agents and robots. It extends technology acceptance ideas to socially interactive systems and is especially useful in HRI and assistive technology contexts.

Source: Heerink, M., Kröse, B., Evers, V., and Wielinga, B. (2010). Assessing Acceptance of Assistive Social Agent Technology by Older Adults: the Almere Model. International Journal of Social Robotics, 2, 361-375. DOI: 10.1007/s12369-010-0068-5

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Social Acceptance: Internet Users' Information Privacy Concerns (IUIPC)
method.p: Description: The IUIPC scale measures concerns about the collection, control, and awareness of personal information online. It is useful in HCI when privacy perceptions are important for understanding acceptance, trust, or adoption of digital systems.

Source: Malhotra, N. K., Kim, S. S., and Agarwal, J. (2004). Internet Users' Information Privacy Concerns (IUIPC): The Construct, the Scale, and a Causal Model. Information Systems Research, 15(4), 336-355. DOI: 10.1287/isre.1040.0032

method.title: Quantitative Subjective User Experience: Visual Aesthetics of Websites Inventory - Short Version (VisAWI-S)
method.p: Description: The VisAWI-S is a short questionnaire for measuring perceived visual aesthetics of websites and related interfaces. It is useful when visual design quality should be assessed separately from usability or general user experience.

Source: Moshagen, M., and Thielsch, M. T. (2013). A short version of the visual aesthetics of websites inventory. Behaviour and Information Technology, 32(12), 1305-1311. DOI: 10.1080/0144929X.2012.694910

method.title: Prototyping Method: Experience Prototyping
method.p: Description: Experience prototyping is a design method that enables designers, researchers, and stakeholders to actively explore and communicate experiences with a future product or service. It is especially useful for interactive, embodied, ubicomp, or XR concepts where static mockups are insufficient.
Source: Buchenau, M., and Suri, J. F. (2000). Experience Prototyping. In Proceedings of the 3rd Conference on Designing Interactive Systems (DIS '00), 424-433. DOI: 10.1145/347642.347802

method.title: Prototyping Method: Wizard of Oz (WoZ)
method.p: Description: Wizard of Oz is a prototyping method in which participants interact with a system that appears autonomous but is partially or fully operated by a hidden human. It is useful for evaluating concepts and interaction flows before the underlying technology has been fully implemented.
Source: Riek, L. D. (2012). Wizard of Oz Studies in HRI: A Systematic Review and New Reporting Guidelines. Journal of Human-Robot Interaction, 1(1), 119-136. DOI: 10.5898/JHRI.1.1.Riek

method.title: Qualitative Feedback: Constructive Interaction / Co-Discovery Learning
method.p: Description: Constructive interaction is a usability evaluation method in which two participants work together on tasks and naturally verbalize their thoughts while interacting. It is often used as an alternative to think-aloud, especially when a more natural conversational style is desired.

Source: van den Haak, M. J., de Jong, M. D. T., and Schellens, P. J. (2004). Employing think-aloud protocols and constructive interaction to test the usability of online library catalogues: a methodological comparison. Interacting with Computers, 16(6), 1153-1170. DOI: 10.1016/j.intcom.2004.07.007

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Presence: Slater-Usoh-Steed Presence Questionnaire (SUS Presence)
method.p: Description: The Slater-Usoh-Steed questionnaire is a classic self-report measure of presence in virtual environments. It is commonly used in VR research when a short and established presence instrument is needed.
Source: Slater, M., Usoh, M., and Steed, A. (1994). Depth of Presence in Virtual Environments. Presence: Teleoperators and Virtual Environments, 3(2), 130-144. DOI: 10.1162/pres.1994.3.2.130
