method.title: Quantitative Subjective User Experience: User Engagement Scale Short Form (UES-SF)
method.p: Description: The User Engagement Scale Short Form (UES-SF) is a shortened version of the original UES for efficient use in studies where questionnaire space is limited. It provides a compact assessment of user engagement while preserving the core dimensions of the original measure. It is especially useful in HCI evaluations that combine several questionnaires in a single study.

Source: O'Brien, H. L., Cairns, P., and Hall, M. (2018). A practical approach to measuring user engagement with the refined user engagement scale (UES) and new UES short form. International Journal of Human-Computer Studies, 112, 28-39. DOI: 10.1016/j.ijhcs.2018.01.004

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Trust: Trust in Automation Scale
method.p: Description: The Trust in Automation Scale is a self-report measure developed to assess the extent to which users trust an automated system. It captures perceptions such as reliability, predictability, dependability, and confidence in automated behavior. In HCI and human-AI interaction, the scale is useful when evaluating whether users appropriately trust or distrust automated support, decision systems, or intelligent interfaces.

Source: Jian, J.-Y., Bisantz, A. M., and Drury, C. G. (2000). Foundations for an empirically determined scale of trust in automated systems. International Journal of Cognitive Ergonomics, 4(1), 53-71. DOI: 10.1207/S15327566IJCE0401_04

method.title: Quantitative Subjective Explainability: System Causability Scale (SCS)
method.p: Description: The System Causability Scale (SCS) is a questionnaire for evaluating the quality of explanations provided by intelligent systems. It focuses on whether an explanation helps users understand causal relations, supports effective reasoning, and is usable in context. The scale is particularly relevant in HCI research on explainable AI, decision support, and human-AI interaction.

Source: Holzinger, A., Carrington, A., and Müller, H. (2020). Measuring the quality of explanations: The System Causability Scale (SCS). KI - Künstliche Intelligenz, 34, 193-198. DOI: 10.1007/s13218-020-00636-z

method.title: Quantitative Objective Accessibility: SAMBA (Semi-Automatic Method for Measuring Barriers of Accessibility)
method.p: Description: SAMBA is a semi-automatic accessibility evaluation method for identifying and quantifying barriers in web interfaces. It combines automated inspection with expert judgment and focuses on the severity and impact of accessibility barriers rather than only reporting rule violations. In HCI, it is useful when researchers want a more structured objective accessibility assessment than a simple pass/fail conformance check.

Source: Brajnik, G., and Lomuscio, R. (2007). SAMBA: a semi-automatic method for measuring barriers of accessibility. In Proceedings of the 9th International ACM SIGACCESS Conference on Computers and Accessibility (ASSETS '07). DOI: 10.1145/1296843.1296853

method.title: Quantitative Objective Accessibility: WCAG Conformance Audit / WCAG-Based Accessibility Evaluation
method.p: Description: A WCAG-based accessibility audit evaluates whether an interface conforms to the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines and is one of the most common objective accessibility methods in HCI and web research. It is especially useful when researchers need a structured expert review of accessibility requirements and want to document conformance, violations, and reliability of judgments in a standardized way.

Source: Brajnik, G., Yesilada, Y., and Harper, S. (2012). Is accessibility conformance an elusive property? A study of validity and reliability of WCAG 2.0. ACM Transactions on Accessible Computing, 4(2), Article 8. DOI: 10.1145/2141943.2141946

method.title: Qualitative Feedback: Team Usability Testing
method.p: Description: Team Usability Testing is a groupware evaluation method for real-time distributed collaboration systems. Unlike individual usability testing, it is designed to uncover team-level usability problems that only occur when several users work together on a shared task. The method combines questionnaires, on-screen behavior recordings, and post-experiment interviews, and is especially useful for CSCW and collaborative software.

Source: Geszten, D., Tóvölgyi, S., and Hercegfi, K. (2021). Empirical study of Team Usability Testing: a laboratory experiment. Cognition, Technology & Work, 23, 575-594. DOI: 10.1007/s10111-020-00647-8

method.title: Data-driven Analysis: Mechanics of Collaboration (MoC) Task Analysis
method.p: Description: The Mechanics of Collaboration is a task-analytic framework for modeling collaborative actions in shared-workspace groupware. It helps researchers analyze what users need to do together, where collaborative breakdowns occur, and which aspects of a system hinder coordination. In HCI, it is particularly useful for the evaluation and redesign of CSCW tools and synchronous collaboration systems.

Source: Pinelle, D., Gutwin, C., and Greenberg, S. (2003). Task analysis for groupware usability evaluation: Modeling shared-workspace tasks with the mechanics of collaboration. ACM Transactions on Computer-Human Interaction, 10(4), 281-311. DOI: 10.1145/966930.966932

method.title: Quantitative Objective Awareness: Situation Awareness Global Assessment Technique (SAGAT)
method.p: Description: The Situation Awareness Global Assessment Technique (SAGAT) is an objective method for measuring situation awareness in dynamic tasks. It typically pauses an ongoing simulation or interactive task and probes the participant's knowledge of the current state, thereby assessing perception, comprehension, and projection. In HCI, SAGAT is relevant for complex interactive systems such as driving, aviation, supervision, XR, and safety-critical interfaces.

Source: Endsley, M. R. (1988). Situation awareness global assessment technique (SAGAT). In Proceedings of the IEEE 1988 National Aerospace and Electronics Conference, 789-795. DOI: 10.1109/NAECON.1988.195097

method.title: Data-driven Analysis: Workspace Awareness Framework
method.p: Description: Workspace awareness refers to the up-to-the-moment understanding of another person's interaction with a shared workspace. The Workspace Awareness Framework provides a structured way to analyze the information people need to coordinate their actions in collaborative systems, such as who is present, where they are working, and what they are doing. In HCI, it is useful for analyzing and designing real-time groupware and collaborative interfaces.
Source: Gutwin, C., and Greenberg, S. (2002). A Descriptive Framework of Workspace Awareness for Real-Time Groupware. Computer Supported Cooperative Work, 11, 411-446. DOI: 10.1023/A:1021271517844

method.title: A Critical Literature Review

measures.method:
Description: Aims to demonstrate writer has extensively researched literature and critically evaluated its quality. Goes beyond mere description to include degree of analysis and conceptual innovation. Typically results in hypothesis or model. Seeks to identify significant items in the field.
Review Guidelines: Click here
Review Time: Up to author
Review Resources: Up to author
Team: One author
Searching strategy: Any or none
Appraisal: No formal quality assessment. Attempts to evaluate according to contribution.
Results synthesis: Typically narrative, perhaps conceptual or chronological.
Analysis: Significant component: seeks to identify conceptual contribution to embody existing or derive new theory.
Grant, M.J. and Booth, A. (2009). A typology of reviews: An analysis of 14 review types and associated methodologies. Health Information and Libraries Journal, 26(2), 91-108.

method.title: A Rapid Literature Review

measures.method:
Description: Assessment of what is already known about a policy or practice issue, by using systematic review methods to search and critically appraise existing research.
Review Guidelines: Click here
Review Time: 2 - 6 months
Review Resources: Up to author
Team: One author
Searching strategy: Any or none
Appraisal: Time-limited. Attempts to evaluate according to contribution.
Results synthesis: Typically narrative and tabular.
Analysis: Quantities of literature and overall quality/direction of effect of literature.
Grant, M.J. and Booth, A. (2009). A typology of reviews: An analysis of 14 review types and associated methodologies. Health Information and Libraries Journal, 26(2), 91-108.

method.title: A State-of-the-Art Literature Review

measures.method:
Description: Tend to address more current matters in contrast to other combined retrospective and current approaches. May offer new perspectives on issue or point out area for further research. Aims for comprehensive searching of current literature.
Review Guidelines: Click here
Review Time: Up to author
Review Resources: Up to author
Team: One author
Searching strategy: Any or none
Appraisal: No formal quality assessment.
Results synthesis: Typically narrative, may have tabular accompaniment.
Analysis: Current state of knowledge and priorities for future investigation and research.
Grant, M.J. and Booth, A. (2009). A typology of reviews: An analysis of 14 review types and associated methodologies. Health Information and Libraries Journal, 26(2), 91-108.

method.title: A Narrative Literature Review

measures.method:
Description: Traditional (narrative) literature reviews provide a broad overview of a research topic with no clear methodological approach. Information is collected and interpreted unsystematically with subjective summaries of findings. Authors aim to describe and discuss the literature from a contextual or theoretical point of view. Although the reviews may be conducted by topic experts, due to preconceived ideas or conclusions, they could be subject to bias. This sort of literature review can be appropriate if you have a broad topic area, are working on your own, or have time constraints.
Review Guidelines: Click here
Review Time: 1 - 4 weeks
Review Resources: Up to author
Team: One or more authors
Searching strategy: Any or none
Appraisal: Any or none
Results synthesis: Narrative commentary
Analysis: Summary of selected articles
Ferrari R. Writing narrative style literature reviews. Medical Writing. 2015 Dec 1;24(4):230-5.
Grant, M.J. and Booth, A. (2009). A typology of reviews: An analysis of 14 review types and associated methodologies. Health Information and Libraries Journal, 26(2), 91-108.

method.title: A Systematic Literature Review: PRISMA Method

measures.method:
Description: PRISMA is an evidence-based minimum set of items for reporting in systematic reviews and meta-analyses. PRISMA primarily focuses on the reporting of reviews evaluating the effects of interventions, but can also be used as a basis for reporting systematic reviews with objectives other than evaluating interventions.
Review Guidelines: Click here
Review Time: 4-6 weeks or more depending on: number of databases, complexity, number of papers
Review Resources: Appropriate research databases for the research question
Team: Two or more people required for screening
Searching strategy: Exhaustive - aiming for all data published
Appraisal: Quality assessment involved
Results synthesis: Typically narrative or tabular
Analysis: what is known; recommendations for practice; what remains unknown; uncertainty around findings; recommendations for future research; taxonomies; classification schemes; methodological overview; ...
PRISMA Statement and Citing PRISMA Grant, M.J. and Booth, A. (2009). A typology of reviews: An analysis of 14 review types and associated methodologies. Health Information and Libraries Journal, 26(2), 91-108.

method.title: A Systematic Literature Review: Kitchenham Procedure

measures.method:
Description: Appropriate for software engineering researchers and a mean of evaluating and interpreting all available research relevant to a particular research question, topic area, or phenomenon of interest. Aim is to present a fair evaluation of a research topic by using a trustworthy, rigorous, and auditable methodology. The guideline presented was derived from three existing guidelines used by medical researchers. The guideline has been adapted to reflect the specific problems of software engineering research. The guideline covers three phases of a systematic review: (1) planning the review, (2) conducting the review and (3) reporting the review. It does not consider the impact of question type on the review procedures, nor does it specify in detail mechanisms needed to undertake meta-analysis.
Review Guidelines: Click here
Review Time: 4-6 weeks or more depending on: number of databases, complexity, number of papers
Review Resources: Appropriate research databases for the research question
Team: Two or more people required for screening
Searching strategy: Exhaustive - aiming for all data published
Appraisal: Quality assessment involved
Results synthesis: Typically narrative or tabular
Analysis: what is known; recommendations for practice; what remains unknown; uncertainty around findings; recommendations for future research; taxonomies; classification schemes; methodological overview; ...
Kitchenham, B. (2004). Procedures for performing systematic reviews. Keele, UK, Keele University, 33(2004), 1-26. Grant, M.J. and Booth, A. (2009). A typology of reviews: An analysis of 14 review types and associated methodologies. Health Information and Libraries Journal, 26(2), 91-108.

method.title: A Systematic Literature Review: The APISSER Methodology

measures.method:
Description: Based on the well-known and proven PRISMA methodology from medical sciences, APISSER adapts and enhances the method to follow a task-oriented engineering flow and to be supported by customized tools. APISSER takes a step forward towards standardizing the methodology for executing systematic literature reviews in engineering by proposing a tool-supported and task-oriented engineering flow methodology to execute systematic literature reviews in engineering.
Review Guidelines: Click here
Review Time: 4-6 weeks or more depending on: number of databases, complexity, number of papers
Review Resources: Appropriate research databases for the research question
Team: One or (better) two people required for screening
Searching strategy: Completeness of searching determined by scope constraints, often broad, often iterative
Appraisal: No formal quality assessment
Results synthesis: Typically tabular with some narrative commentary
Analysis: Characterizes quantity and breadth of literature. Attempts to specify the viability of more focused reviews
Castillo, S., and Grbovic, P. (2022). IEEE Access, 10, 23700-23707. The APISSER Methodology for Systematic Literature Reviews in Engineering, 13(3), 141-146. Grant, M.J. and Booth, A. (2009). A typology of reviews: An analysis of 14 review types and associated methodologies. Health Information and Libraries Journal, 26(2), 91-108.

method.title: A Scoping Review: The JBI Method

measures.method:
Description: The JBI guidance for scoping reviews includes additional guidance on several methodological issues, such as when a scoping review is (or is not) appropriate, and how to extract, analyze, and present results, and provides clarification for implications for practice and research. Furthermore, it is aligned with the PRISMA-ScR to ensure consistent reporting.
Review Guidelines: Click here
Review Time: 4-6 weeks or more depending on: number of databases, complexity, number of papers
Review Resources: Appropriate research databases for the research question
Team: One or (better) two people required for screening
Searching strategy: Completeness of searching determined by scope constraints, often broad, often iterative
Appraisal: No formal quality assessment
Results synthesis: Typically tabular with some narrative commentary
Analysis: Characterizes quantity and breadth of literature. Attempts to specify the viability of more focused reviews
JBI Review Guide Grant, M.J. and Booth, A. (2009). A typology of reviews: An analysis of 14 review types and associated methodologies. Health Information and Libraries Journal, 26(2), 91-108.

method.title: A Scoping Review: The PRISMA-Sc Method

measures.method:
Description: PRISMA-SC is a PRISMA extension for scoping reviews. The checklist contains 20 essential reporting items and 2 optional items to include when completing a scoping review. Scoping reviews serve to synthesize evidence and assess the scope of literature on a topic. Among other objectives, scoping reviews help determine whether a systematic review of the literature is warranted.
Review Guidelines: Click here
Review Time: 4-6 weeks or more depending on: number of databases, complexity, number of papers
Review Resources: Appropriate research databases for the research question
Team: One or (better) two people required for screening
Searching strategy: Completeness of searching determined by scope constraints, often broad, often iterative
Appraisal: No formal quality assessment
Results synthesis: Typically tabular with some narrative commentary
Analysis: Characterizes quantity and breadth of literature. Attempts to specify the viability of more focused reviews
PRISMA Statement and Citing PRISMA Grant, M.J. and Booth, A. (2009). A typology of reviews: An analysis of 14 review types and associated methodologies. Health Information and Libraries Journal, 26(2), 91-108.

method.title: A Meta-Analysis: The PRISMA Method

measures.method:
Description: PRISMA is an evidence-based minimum set of items for reporting in systematic reviews and meta-analyses. PRISMA primarily focuses on the reporting of reviews evaluating the effects of interventions, but can also be used as a basis for reporting systematic reviews with objectives other than evaluating interventions.
Review Guidelines: Click here
Review Time: 4-6 weeks or more depending on: number of databases, complexity, number of papers
Review Resources: Appropriate research databases for the research question
Team: Two or more people required for screening
Searching strategy: Exhaustive - aiming for all data published
Appraisal: Quality assessment involved
Results synthesis: Typically narrative or tabular
Analysis: what is known; recommendations for practice; what remains unknown; uncertainty around findings; recommendations for future research; taxonomies; classification schemes; methodological overview; ...
PRISMA Statement and Citing PRISMA Grant, M.J. and Booth, A. (2009). A typology of reviews: An analysis of 14 review types and associated methodologies. Health Information and Libraries Journal, 26(2), 91-108.
